fields.definition_origin: Two convergent frameworks. (1) Hick's Law (Hick-Hyman Law) — William E. Hick (1952) 'On the rate of gain of information' (Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology 4:11-26) and Ray Hyman (1953) 'Stimulus information as a determinant of reaction time'. Decision time scales logarithmically with the number of equiprobable choices: RT = a + b·log2(n+1). Replicated thousands of times (Proctor & Schneider 2018 review). (2) Decision Fatigue — Baumeister, Bratslavsky, Muraven & Tice (1998) 'Ego depletion: Is the active self a limited resource?' (Journal of Personality and Social Psychology 74:1252-1265): repeated decisions deplete a finite cognitive resource, reducing later decision quality and willpower. Vohs et al. (2008) 'Making choices impairs subsequent self-control' applied to consumer choice. Onboarding application via NN/g, Apple HIG, and Material 3 Expressive choice-cap guidelines.
fields.core_mechanism: Each onboarding question or paywall option imposes two costs: (1) Hick's Law decision time per screen (logarithmic in choice count — 5 options is meaningfully slower than 3, but only marginally faster than 7); (2) Decision Fatigue accumulating across the flow (each decision drains resource for the next). Compounded: a 33-step questionnaire with 4 options per step (~132 micro-decisions) approaches the depletion limit by step 30 — exactly when the paywall lands. Workaround: (a) cap choices per screen at 3-4 (Hick's Law), (b) prepopulate defaults (decision pre-made — only ratify or change), (c) recommend a default with visual highlight ('Most popular'), (d) chunk decisions across screens (one per screen, big-button conversational), (e) order decisions from low-stakes to high-stakes (preference before identity before goal before paywall). Critical paywall application: 3-4 plans max with one highlighted as default — 5+ plans tank conversion (Decision Paralysis is a stronger version of Hick's Law). The decoy effect (3 plans with middle = bait, top = real target) is a deliberate Hick's Law manipulation.
fields.neuroscience_correlate: Hick-Hyman effects map to cognitive control network (dorsolateral PFC, anterior cingulate cortex) — Wu et al. (2018, PMC5998988) showed RT scaling correlates with ACC engagement on choice difficulty. Decision Fatigue engages prefrontal-glucose-depletion (Gailliot et al. 2007 — though replication of glucose mechanism is contested in newer literature; the behavioral phenomenon is robust). Default-bias and choice-architecture effects engage ventral striatum (Nicolle et al. 2011) — pre-selected options are processed more fluently and feel more rewarding.
fields.empirical_evidence: (1) Hick (1952) original n=2 (small) replicated thousands of times in HCI; Proctor & Schneider (2018) review confirms log relationship holds across 60+ years with caveats on practice and stimulus-response compatibility. (2) Hick-Hyman fMRI mapping — Wu et al. 2018 (PMC5998988). (3) Baumeister et al. (1998) ego-depletion original — though the 'glucose' explanation has been contested (Hagger et al. 2010 meta + Carter et al. 2015 multi-lab failure to replicate exact mechanism), the *behavioral* phenomenon of decision fatigue is robust. (4) Vohs et al. (2008) — 4 experiments on choice depletion; effect size moderate. (5) Iyengar & Lepper (2000) 'When Choice is Demotivating' — jam study (24 vs 6 jam options): 24-option display had 60% browse rate but only 3% buy rate; 6-option display had 40% browse rate but 30% buy rate. Choice paralysis effect ~10x. (6) Industry validation: NN/g, Apple HIG (≤4 tab-bar items), Material 3 Expressive choice-cap research. (7) Adapty 2026: paywalls with 3-4 plans convert better than 5+ plans (Decision Paralysis is the dominant variable). (8) Kleeve PDF research base: Hick's Law is critical for paywall plan-selection psychology; 5+ plans tank conversion. (9) Counter-evidence: Linear / Raycast Cmd+K command palettes successfully expose 200+ choices because the search-rank surface collapses Hick's Law — but this only works for power users, not onboarding.
fields.measurable_outcome_metric: Time-to-decision per screen (target <3s for big-button onboarding screens), abandonment-by-screen ratio, paywall plan-selection time (target <8s), paywall plan-selection abandonment, error-rate per screen (proxy for fatigue), late-flow decision quality (D7 retention regressed on questionnaire-completion-time), trial activation rate by paywall plan-count cohort.
fields.conversion_lift_benchmark: Iyengar & Lepper (2000) jam study: 6-option vs 24-option display — 10x conversion difference. Paywall plan-count optimization: industry teardowns (Adapty 2026, RevenueCat 2025) suggest 3-4 plans convert ~15-25% better than 5+ plans, with one highlighted as default adding additional ~5-10%. Iyengar et al. (2004) 401(k) participation: each additional fund option reduced participation by ~2%. Hick's Law in HCI: decision time grows by ~150ms per doubled-option-set in well-designed interfaces (Proctor & Schneider 2018 review).
fields.engineering_tactics: (1) Hard-cap visible choices at ≤4 per onboarding question (3 typical, 5 absolute max for category-of-N picker). (2) Hard-cap plan-selection paywall at 3-4 plans. (3) Default highlighting: pre-select the recommended option (annual plan with 'Most popular' badge, Regular goal with default radio). (4) Prepopulated values where reasonable: birthdate from device locale, units from device locale, language from system. (5) One question per screen — never compound decisions on a single screen (ego-depletion accumulates faster on shared screens). (6) Sequencing: low-stakes → high-stakes (preference before identity before painpoint before paywall) — the paywall arrives at peak depletion, so the default plan must be the easy 'I'll just take the recommended one' choice. (7) Skip/default-accept affordances: 'Skip' and 'Use suggested' visible without scroll. (8) For long flows, group questions into sections that share a context — section transitions act as cognitive resets. (9) Decoy plan structure: 3 plans where middle = bait, top-anchored or default = real target (anchoring + decoy + Hick's Law combine). (10) Big-button conversational pattern: each option is a tap target ≥96dp tall, full-width, with sensoryFeedback .selection — physical tap commitment also locks the decision (sunk-cost link).
fields.real_world_examples: (1) Cal AI questionnaire — every screen has 3-4 big-button options; for the body-fat percentage screen, instead of a slider with 100 options, 4 visual reference cards (low/medium/high/very-high). (2) Headspace onboarding — meditation experience picker has 4 levels (Beginner / Some experience / Regular / Daily); not a slider. (3) Duolingo language picker — paginated by region rather than 100-option flat list (chunks Hick's Law). (4) Cal AI paywall — 2 plans (annual highlighted with 'Save 67%' + 'Most popular' badge / monthly), default annual selected. (5) Apple Vision Pro onboarding gesture demo — 4 distinct gestures presented one at a time (chunked Hick's). (6) Notion template picker — categorized by type (Personal / Team / Project) instead of flat 200-template list. (7) Anti-example: a paywall with Free / Monthly / 3-month / 6-month / Annual / Lifetime / Family / Student — 8 plans = Decision Paralysis, conversion craters. (8) Anti-example: gender picker with 12 options on screen 1 — Hick's Law + sensitivity violation; better as identity question after warmup.
fields.mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile is more Hick-sensitive: smaller viewport amplifies any list of choices, thumb travel adds physical cost to extra options, attention is more divided. Apple HIG: ≤4 tab-bar items. Material: ≤5 bottom-nav items. Web tolerates more parallel choices and benefits from command-palette mitigations (Cmd+K) unavailable on mobile. Web equivalent: still cap visible primary choices ≤5 above the fold, hide rest in 'View all' progressive disclosure. Mobile-primary for the big-button conversational pattern.
fields.platform_design_language_alignment: Strongly aligned with both. Apple HIG explicitly: 'Limit the number of choices people have to make' (Aesthetic Integrity), ≤4 tab bar items. Material 3 Expressive (I/O 2025, 18,000-participant study) — choice-capped expressive UIs achieve 4x faster element recognition vs unbounded layouts. Liquid Glass and M3E both treat default-highlighting as a first-class motion + material primitive (the recommended option visually pops). Both platforms make Hick discipline ship-faster guidance.
fields.category_fit: Universal — every category benefits. Critical for: paywall design (the highest-stakes Hick's Law application — 3-4 plans max), onboarding questionnaire (one question per screen ≤4 options), AI-wrapper apps (chat-first apps risk overloading first message with 8 example prompts — chunk to 3). Less critical: pure-utility (calculator already minimal). DO NOT use 5+ plans on any 2026 paywall (Decision Paralysis + Apple Guideline 3.1.2 toggle ban together push toward 2-3 side-by-side plans).
fields.tooling_options: Adapty (paywall builder with plan-count templates, 3-plan side-by-side default), Superwall (paywall A/B with plan-count variants), RevenueCat (offering / package configuration capped at 3-4), Plotline (one-question-per-screen onboarding), PostHog (decision-time per-screen analytics + paywall plan-count A/B test), UXCam (heatmap on paywall plan options).
fields.ai_dependency: none: Hick's Law and decision-fatigue mitigation are pure design/architecture; no LLM call required. Optional: small-model classifier in CI to flag paywall configurations with >4 plans or onboarding screens with >5 visible choices.
fields.claude_design_application: Instruct Claude to apply numeric caps and pre-selected defaults. Example fragment 1: 'Build the daily-goal-picker onboarding screen. Hard caps: 4 big-button options (Casual 5min / Regular 15min / Serious 30min / Insane 60min), each 96dp tall, full-width, brand-color border on selected, default selection on Regular pre-rendered. Skip CTA visible top-right. Continue CTA pinned bottom thumb-zone, 56dp tall. Forbid >5 options. Microcopy: \'You can change this anytime.\' For the paywall: 2 plans (Annual highlighted with \'Most popular\' badge + \'Save 67%\' anchor / Monthly), default annual pre-selected, side-by-side layout (post-2026-01 toggle ban). Forbid 3+ plans on the paywall.' Example fragment 2: 'Audit every onboarding screen and the paywall: count visible interactive choices. Reject builds where any onboarding screen exceeds 5 choices or the paywall exceeds 4 plans. Output a screen → choice-count audit comment block.'
fields.stitch_application: Stitch needs explicit numeric caps and default highlighting because it produces unbounded choice lists by default. Example fragment 1: 'Mobile onboarding goal-picker, 4 options ONLY: Casual 5 min, Regular 15 min, Serious 30 min, Insane 60 min. Vertical stack, 16dp gap, each card 96dp tall, full-width-minus-32dp, 12px corner radius, off-white background. Default selection: Regular has brand-color (#FF6B47) 2px border AND brand-color icon (vs grey on others). Helper text below stack: \'You can change this anytime.\' in 14pt Manrope grey. Forbid 5th option, forbid slider, forbid free-text input.' Example fragment 2: 'Mobile paywall with 2 plans side-by-side (Annual/Monthly), Annual on left with brand-color 2px border + \'Most popular\' pill badge top-center + \'Save 67%\' anchor copy below price. Monthly on right, neutral border. Buy CTA pinned bottom thumb-zone. Forbid 3+ plans, forbid toggle (banned 2026-01).'
fields.ai_codegen_failure_mode: AI codegen defaults to: (1) pasting all available options into a single dropdown (long flat list violates Hick's); (2) paywall with 4-6 plans because 'comprehensive'; (3) no pre-selected default ('let user choose freely'); (4) 5+ category options on the first onboarding question; (5) toggle paywall (now banned 2026-01); (6) sliders for what should be 4 discrete choices. Counter-prompt: 'Cap visible choices at ≤4 per onboarding screen, ≤4 plans on the paywall. Pre-select a recommended default with brand-color border + badge. Forbid sliders for what should be discrete options. Forbid toggle paywall (Apple 3.1.2 banned 2026-01) — use side-by-side plan selection. Output a choice-count audit comment block before writing JSX.'
fields.anti_patterns: (1) Paywall with 5+ plans (Decision Paralysis tanks conversion). (2) Toggle paywall (banned 2026-01 by Apple Guideline 3.1.2). (3) Long flat dropdown for what should be 4 big-button options. (4) No default highlighting on paywall (forces full Hick's Law calculus at peak depletion moment). (5) Compound questions on a single screen (ego-depletion accumulates). (6) High-stakes question on screen 1 (decision quality is best at session start; reserve the paywall for end). (7) 'Customize every setting' Day-0 screen (20 toggles before user has earned context). (8) Inconsistent option count across questionnaire (4 options on step 1, 12 on step 5 — breaks rhythm). (9) Hick's Law violation in onboarding-into-empty-state (33-question onboarding into a blank dashboard with 50 features = decision-fatigued user can't even start).
fields.regulatory_risk: Medium. (1) Decoy-effect paywalls with deliberate bait-and-switch plan structures are dark-pattern radar (EU Digital Fairness Act Q4 2026 draft, FTC). (2) Default-pre-selected upgrades to higher-priced plans without clear opt-out are FTC Click-to-Cancel risk. (3) 5+ plans designed to confuse rather than inform are now flagged. (4) Apple Guideline 3.1.2 effective 2026-01 — toggle paywalls banned (a specific Hick's Law violation). Mitigate: cap plans at 3-4, default to clearly the user's interest (annual savings), make 'Maybe later' visible, no pre-checked upgrade boxes.
fields.platform_constraints_2026: Apple Guideline 3.1.2 effective 2026-01 — toggle paywalls BANNED, must use side-by-side plan selection or transparent timeline. Apple HIG ≤4 tab-bar items. Material ≤5 bottom-nav items. WCAG 2.2 §3.3.7 redundant entry — don't ask the same decision twice across steps. Apple Guideline 4.0 — design polish; cluttered choice lists have been cited in rejections. EU Digital Fairness Act (Q4 2026 draft) flags manipulative decoy plans.
fields.accessibility_implications: Choice-capping helps users with cognitive impairment, ADHD, screen-reader users (fewer items to traverse). Default highlighting reduces cognitive load — but must be VoiceOver/TalkBack readable as 'selected, recommended'. Touch targets ≥44×44pt iOS / 48dp Android, also ≥24×24px (WCAG 2.2 §2.5.8). Big-button options must have sufficient contrast (4.5:1 text on background, WCAG 2.2 §1.4.3). Long flat lists are screen-reader-hostile (no chunking) — paginate or section. Sliders for what should be discrete choices are hostile to motor-impairment users.
fields.pitfalls_caveats: (1) Hick's Law has caveats: practice, stimulus-response compatibility, and category structure all moderate it (Proctor & Schneider 2018). (2) Decision-fatigue ego-depletion mechanism is contested in replication literature (Hagger 2010 meta + Carter 2015 multi-lab failed to replicate glucose model) — but the behavioral phenomenon (worse late-flow decisions) is robust regardless of mechanism. (3) Over-simplification can hide options users actually want — A/B test, don't assume. (4) Default highlighting can cross into dark-pattern territory if it pre-selects more expensive option; use defaults that favor user interest. (5) Cultural calibration: CN/JP markets tolerate denser choice surfaces; cap-of-4 may feel under-detailed (test). (6) Power-user tension: Linear/Raycast collapse 200+ choices via Cmd+K command palette — not applicable to onboarding but worth noting for power-user paths. (7) Decoy plans are effective but brittle — if user notices the decoy, trust collapses.
fields.cross_cultural_validity: Hick's Law and decision-fatigue are biologically grounded (cognitive control network) and replicate broadly. Cultural calibration on tolerance for choice density: Western markets (US, UK, AU) prefer minimal-choice interfaces; East Asian markets (CN, JP, KR) tolerate denser choice surfaces (Yahoo Japan, Naver, Tmall) without subjective overload. Iyengar & Lepper's jam study has been replicated cross-culturally with effect-size variance. Practical: ship 3-4 plans default, A/B test 5-plan variant in CN/JP/KR. RTL languages: button order and default position must mirror. Pricing currency localization is itself a Hick decision — pre-render in user's locale to remove cognitive currency-conversion load.
fields.prescribed_action: Cap visible choices at 3-4 per onboarding question and 3-4 plans on the paywall, pre-select a recommended default with brand-color border + 'Most popular' badge, sequence questions low-stakes → high-stakes, and forbid the toggle paywall (banned 2026-01) in favor of side-by-side plan selection. Audit every screen for choice count and reject builds exceeding the cap.
fields.questionnaire_relevance: Determines questionnaire option-count ceiling: every question screen has ≤4 big-button options. For categories that genuinely span >4 (e.g., job role, country, age), use chunked categorization or 'Other' affordance. Sequencing rule: low-stakes (preference) before identity before goal before painpoint before paywall — decision-fatigue reservoir is full at start, depleted at end; paywall is the most important late decision so it must be the EASIEST one (default pre-selected, minimal options, clear value anchor).
fields.aha_relevance: Hick's Law discipline preserves the cognitive bandwidth required to FEEL the Aha moment. A user who has burned through 132 micro-decisions cannot emotionally register the AI plan reveal — they're depleted. The questionnaire must offer enough structure (defaults, big-button, low option count) that the user arrives at step N-1 with reservoir intact. Conversely, the AI plan reveal is itself a non-decision moment (the system decides for the user) — relief from decision-load amplifies the peak.
fields.emotional_archetype_alignment: Universal — choice discipline applies to every archetype. Strongest fit: Sage (clear curated knowledge, no clutter), Caregiver (gentle no-pressure choice with default), Magician (concentrated revelation needs concentrated choice). Conflicts with Maximalist Jester aesthetics that rely on visual density — even maximalist must respect per-screen choice cap. Pairs with .agent/research/emotional-design/results/Cognitive_Load_Hicks_Law_Gestalt.json.
fields.anti_pattern_paired: Decision Paralysis (5+ plans paywall); Toggle Paywall (banned 2026-01); Wall-of-Text (deck-of-cards carousel forces decisions on every card); 'Customize every setting' Day-0 (decision-fatigue overload at worst moment); Decorative Sliders (sliders for what should be 4 discrete options).
sources: [Hick (1952) — On the rate of gain of information (Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology)](https://doi.org/10.1080/17470215208416600)
[Hick-Hyman Law mediated by cognitive control network — PMC 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5998988/)
[Iyengar & Lepper (2000) — When Choice is Demotivating (Journal of Personality and Social Psychology)](https://faculty.washington.edu/jdb/345/345%20Articles/Iyengar%20%26%20Lepper%20(2000).pdf)
